title: Cat Food Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate daily cat food portions based on weight and activity. Get personalized feeding recommendations. Free, fast, and updated for 2026.
intro: This calculator estimates the ideal daily food portion for your cat in grams, based on body weight and activity level. The core formula uses your cat's metabolic energy requirement (MER), derived from its Resting Energy Requirement (RER = 70 × weight_kg^0.75 kcal/day), then multiplied by an activity factor (1.0–1.8). The resulting calorie target is divided by the caloric density of the food (typically 3.0–4.5 kcal/g for dry kibble, 0.8–1.2 kcal/g for wet food) to get the daily gram amount. Use this tool when starting a new diet, managing weight, or transitioning between life stages.
keyTakeaway: **Daily grams = (70 × weight_kg^0.75 × activity_factor) ÷ kcal_per_gram_of_food** — e.g., a 4 kg neutered adult cat needs ~(70 × 4^0.75 × 1.2) ÷ 3.5 ≈ **96 g/day** of standard dry kibble (~350 kcal/100g).
useCases: Calculating the exact daily kibble portion (in grams) for a newly adopted adult cat to avoid overfeeding from day one.
Adjusting food quantity after spaying or neutering, when a cat's energy needs drop by roughly 20–30% compared to an intact adult.
Determining a reduced-calorie portion for an overweight cat using its target ideal weight rather than its current weight to set a safe caloric deficit.
Scaling up food intake for a pregnant or lactating queen, whose MER multiplier rises to 2.0–3.0× RER depending on the week of gestation or nursing stage.
Cross-checking manufacturer feeding guidelines on a bag of kibble against science-based MER calculations, which often differ significantly.
Switching from dry to wet food and recalculating grams because wet food is ~75% water and has roughly 3–4× fewer kcal per gram than dry food.
explanation:
## How It Is Calculated

The calculation follows the **National Research Council (NRC) feline energy standard**, used by veterinary nutritionists worldwide.

**Step 1 — Resting Energy Requirement (RER)**
```
RER (kcal/day) = 70 × (body_weight_kg ^ 0.75)
```
This is the energy a cat needs at complete rest to maintain basic physiological functions (heart, breathing, thermoregulation).

**Step 2 — Metabolic Energy Requirement (MER)**
```
MER (kcal/day) = RER × activity_factor
```

**Step 3 — Daily Food Amount**
```
Daily_grams = MER ÷ food_caloric_density_kcal_per_gram
```

**Example — 4 kg neutered indoor adult cat, standard dry kibble (350 kcal/100 g = 3.5 kcal/g):**
```
RER  = 70 × (4 ^ 0.75) = 70 × 2.828 = 197.9 kcal/day
MER  = 197.9 × 1.2 (neutered adult) = 237.5 kcal/day
Food = 237.5 ÷ 3.5 = 67.8 g/day ≈ 68 g
```

---

## Reference Table

### Activity / Life-Stage Multipliers (NRC 2006)

| Cat Profile | MER Multiplier |
|---|---|
| Kitten (< 4 months) | 3.0 × RER |
| Kitten (4–12 months) | 2.5 × RER |
| Intact young adult (1–2 yr) | 1.6 × RER |
| Neutered/spayed adult | 1.2 × RER |
| Active intact adult | 1.6 × RER |
| Sedentary / obese-prone | 1.0 × RER |
| Weight loss (controlled) | 0.8 × RER |
| Pregnant queen (late gestation) | 2.0 × RER |
| Lactating queen (peak, 4 wks) | 3.0 × RER |
| Senior cat (> 11 yr) | 1.1–1.4 × RER |

### Typical Caloric Density of Commercial Cat Foods

| Food Type | kcal / 100 g | kcal / cup (~100g dry) |
|---|---|---|
| Dry kibble (economy) | 300–320 kcal | ~310 kcal |
| Dry kibble (premium) | 350–400 kcal | ~370 kcal |
| Semi-moist pouches | 200–280 kcal | — |
| Wet canned (pâté) | 80–120 kcal | — |
| Wet canned (chunks in gravy) | 60–90 kcal | — |
| Raw frozen (commercial) | 130–180 kcal | — |

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Healthy 5 kg indoor neutered male, premium dry kibble (380 kcal/100 g)
```
RER  = 70 × (5 ^ 0.75) = 70 × 3.344 = 234.1 kcal/day
MER  = 234.1 × 1.2 = 280.9 kcal/day
Food = 280.9 ÷ 3.80 = 73.9 g/day ≈ 74 g
```
Split into **two meals of ~37 g** for better digestion and hunger management.

### Case 2 — Overweight 7 kg cat, target ideal weight 5 kg, weight-loss kibble (310 kcal/100 g)
```
RER (target) = 70 × (5 ^ 0.75) = 234.1 kcal/day
MER (loss)   = 234.1 × 0.8 = 187.3 kcal/day
Food         = 187.3 ÷ 3.10 = 60.4 g/day ≈ 60 g
```
> ⚠️ Always use the **target weight**, not the current weight, when calculating for weight loss.

### Case 3 — 3-month-old kitten, 1.2 kg, wet food pâté (100 kcal/100 g)
```
RER  = 70 × (1.2 ^ 0.75) = 70 × 1.147 = 80.3 kcal/day
MER  = 80.3 × 3.0 = 240.9 kcal/day
Food = 240.9 ÷ 1.00 = 240.9 g/day ≈ 241 g (split across 3–4 meals)
```
Kittens have dramatically higher energy needs per kilogram than adults.

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Using current weight for overweight cats** — This overestimates calories. Always use the *ideal target weight* for the RER calculation when the cat needs to lose weight, or you'll feed 20–40% too many calories.

2. **Ignoring food caloric density** — A premium kibble at 400 kcal/100 g requires ~25% fewer grams per day than an economy kibble at 300 kcal/100 g for the same calorie target. Relying only on volume or cup measures leads to systematic error.

3. **Not accounting for neutering** — Spaying/neutering reduces a cat's MER by approximately 20–30%. Continuing to feed the pre-surgery amount is one of the leading causes of feline obesity; the multiplier drops from ~1.6 to ~1.2.

4. **Mixing wet and dry without recalculating** — Wet food contains ~70–80% moisture and delivers far fewer calories per gram. If you supplement 50% of calories with wet food, the gram amounts from each food type must be calculated separately and summed.

5. **Treating manufacturer guidelines as exact** — Bag feeding guides are intentionally conservative overestimates (they want you to use more product) and are typically formulated for intact, moderately active cats. They do not adjust for your specific cat's life stage or metabolism.

6. **Forgetting treats in the daily calorie budget** — A single commercial cat treat can contain 2–5 kcal. Ten treats/day adds 20–50 kcal — up to 20% of a sedentary indoor cat's entire calorie budget.

---

## Related Calculators

faq.q: How many grams of dry food should I feed my cat per day?
faq.a: It depends on body weight and activity level. A typical 4 kg neutered indoor adult needs roughly 60–80 g/day of standard premium dry kibble (~350–380 kcal/100 g). Using the NRC formula: RER = 70 × (4^0.75) ≈ 198 kcal; MER = 198 × 1.2 ≈ 238 kcal; divide by 3.6 kcal/g = ~66 g/day. Always verify against the caloric density printed on your specific food bag.

faq.q: What is RER and why does it matter for cats?
faq.a: RER stands for Resting Energy Requirement — the calories a cat burns doing absolutely nothing (breathing, heartbeat, organ function). It's calculated as 70 × weight_kg^0.75 (kcal/day), based on metabolic scaling laws established by the National Research Council (NRC 2006). All life-stage and activity adjustments multiply from this baseline, so getting the RER right is the most critical step in any feeding calculation.

faq.q: Does spaying or neutering change how much I should feed my cat?
faq.a: Yes, significantly. Neutered and spayed cats have a metabolic energy requirement multiplier of approximately 1.2× RER, compared to 1.6× for intact adults of the same weight and age. That's a ~25% reduction in daily calorie needs. Failing to adjust food quantity after the surgery is one of the most common causes of feline obesity, which affects an estimated 30–35% of pet cats in the US according to veterinary surveys.

faq.q: Should I feed my cat once or twice a day?
faq.a: Most veterinary nutritionists recommend dividing the daily ration into **2 meals** for adult cats. This helps stabilize blood glucose, reduces begging behavior, and prevents overeating in free-fed situations. Kittens under 6 months should receive 3–4 smaller meals per day due to their higher metabolic rate and smaller stomach capacity. Timed meals also make it easier to monitor appetite changes that may signal illness.

faq.q: How do I calculate food for an overweight cat?
faq.a: For weight loss, calculate RER using the cat's **target ideal weight**, not its current weight, then apply a weight-loss multiplier of 0.8× RER. For example, a 7 kg cat with an ideal weight of 5 kg: RER (target) = 70 × (5^0.75) = 234 kcal/day; MER = 234 × 0.8 = 187 kcal/day. Divide by the caloric density of a weight-management food (typically 290–320 kcal/100 g). Safe weight loss in cats is 0.5–2% of body weight per week; faster loss risks hepatic lipidosis.

faq.q: How does wet food differ from dry kibble in portion size?
faq.a: Wet canned food typically contains 70–80% water and delivers only 80–120 kcal per 100 g, compared to 300–400 kcal per 100 g for dry kibble. This means a cat requiring 240 kcal/day needs only ~65 g of dry food but roughly **200–300 g of wet pâté** to meet the same energy target. Always use kcal/g (printed on the label as 'Metabolizable Energy') rather than grams alone when comparing different food formats.

faq.q: Do senior cats need more or less food than adults?
faq.a: It depends on the senior life stage. Cats aged 7–11 years (mature adult) often become less active and may need 10–20% fewer calories (MER ≈ 1.1–1.2× RER). However, cats over 11 years old frequently experience reduced nutrient absorption and muscle mass loss (sarcopenia), sometimes requiring *more* protein and even slightly higher calorie intake. The NRC 2006 guidelines recommend individualized assessment for senior cats rather than a single universal reduction.

faq.q: Are manufacturer feeding guidelines on cat food bags accurate?
faq.a: Manufacturer guidelines are a starting point, not a precise prescription. They are typically formulated for an intact, moderately active cat and are often set slightly high (to increase product consumption). Studies comparing manufacturer guidelines to NRC-calculated needs show discrepancies of 15–30% in many cases. Always cross-check with the MER formula using your cat's actual weight and life stage, and adjust based on body condition score (BCS) every 2–4 weeks.

faq.q: What caloric density should I use if the food label doesn't show kcal/g?
faq.a: If the label only shows a Guaranteed Analysis (% protein, fat, moisture, fiber), you can estimate metabolizable energy using the modified Atwater factors: protein = 3.5 kcal/g, fat = 8.5 kcal/g, carbohydrate = 3.5 kcal/g. However, the most reliable approach is to look for the ME (Metabolizable Energy) value stated as 'kcal/kg' on the label or the brand's website, then convert: kcal/g = stated kcal/kg ÷ 1000.
